title: Let Us Lay Today the Foundation for Tomorrow's Victory!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. Anna's English sentence is retained with its source capitalization. The Tamil reading copy restores the scan-confirmed proverb `Blood is thicker than Water`, corrects `ஜஐமக்காளத்திற்குள்` to `ஜமக்காளத்திற்குள்`, and repairs unambiguous OCR splits such as `ஆளுங்கட் சியினர்`, `ஒவ் வொரு`, `பன் மடங்கு`, `உடன் பிறப்புகள்`, and `இயக்கத் திற்கும்`. Printed highlighted passages repeated later in the letter are preserved. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: Beginning tomorrow morning, Monday, 22 February 2016, we will meet through interviews at the Kazhagam office in Anna Arivalayam, Chennai, those who have submitted applications to headquarters seeking to contest on behalf of the Kazhagam in the fifteenth general election to the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly. The General Secretary, Professor; Treasurer M. K. Stalin; Principal Secretary Duraimurugan; Organisation Secretary R. S. Bharathi; and I are eager to learn and assess the situation in each constituency and each applicant's prospects of victory.

Interviews will be held on the morning of the 22nd for Kanyakumari, Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi and Ramanathapuram districts, and that afternoon for Virudhunagar, Theni and Dindigul. On the morning of the 23rd, they will cover Sivaganga, Madurai Rural, Madurai City and Erode districts, and that afternoon Coimbatore, Coimbatore City, Salem and Tiruppur. On the morning of the 24th, they will cover Pudukkottai, Namakkal and Tiruchi districts, and that afternoon Karur, Perambalur and Ariyalur. On the morning of the 25th, they will cover Nagapattinam, Tiruvarur, Thanjavur and Cuddalore districts, and that afternoon Villupuram, Krishnagiri, Dharmapuri and the Nilgiris. On the morning of the 26th, they will cover Tiruvannamalai, Vellore and Kanchipuram districts, and that afternoon Tiruvallur and Chennai. On the morning of the 27th, interviews will be held for Puducherry and Karaikal in the Union Territory of Puducherry. Our General Secretary has already announced this schedule.

Applications were accepted from 24 January through 9 February, bringing Rs 12 crore, 97 lakh and 77 thousand into headquarters. Applications have been received from 5,648 people seeking to contest. It has already been made clear that those attending the interviews will not be permitted to bring all their supporters with them.

Everyone who submitted an application has laboured for the Kazhagam; everyone has worked for it in one way or another; and many have taken part in struggles conducted by the Kazhagam and gone to prison. There is not the slightest doubt about that. But, as I have already said, we can select only one person to represent the Kazhagam in each constituency. Several people may be qualified to contest there on its behalf, while one among them may have a greater prospect of victory. You know very well how difficult it is to select such a person, doing so “like a balance that weighs impartially.” A good opportunity will come to everyone. One must not forget that success comes to the person who has learned to wait until their turn arrives. Anna expressed this beautifully in English: “We should learn to Wait till our Turn comes.”

Was that all Anna said? Did he not also give us this analogy? “A bureau may be filled with any number of silk saris. If I choose this particular coloured silk sari for the wedding I am attending today, I will choose another sari when I attend another wedding. Wearing this one today does not mean that I dislike all the others.” Can we forget it?
My appeal to all who submitted applications is this: this is an election in which our Kazhagam must win a majority of the seats.

Applicants must not think merely that they themselves should win this election. They must hold the unshakeable conviction that the DMK must win and that the Rising Sun—the symbol envisioned by Anna, given by Anna and made victorious by Anna—must triumph. Those who receive the opportunity to contest must not adopt the negative attitude, “Now that we have been given the chance, we can ‘take care of’ those who applied against us.” They must embrace everyone with affection, bring them together and labour with their whole breath for the Kazhagam's victory.

Which parties will be our allies, and which seats they will contest as part of the alliance, have yet to be decided. Merely because interviews have been held for a constituency, one cannot assume that the DMK will contest it on behalf of our alliance. If, forgetting the scale of this important task and treating it carelessly, our own Udanpirappugal begin clashing with one another in a few places even before the election, certain people in the media, such as Vaithi and Varadhan, may be driven by their burning resentment to hide from the people's minds the atrocities and grave wrongs of the AIADMK rulers and instead inflate our internal party disputes.

Those few are either limbs of dominant forces or supporters of those forces in expectation of various benefits. They care nothing for journalistic ethics and nothing for the suffering inflicted on the public. They regard a field of service as a field of commerce. If an entire pillar falls in the opposition camp, they refuse to show even a speck of its dust; if a speck of dust rises among us, they spin a story that an entire pillar has collapsed.

Continuing the taste they acquired during the parliamentary election, the ruling party has once again caught certain election officials and put them in its pocket, adding lakhs of bogus voters. Do these people point, even in some obscure corner, to that, to the overflowing allegations of bribery and corruption, or to actions against the public welfare? Do they not struggle desperately to roll everything up and hide it beneath the carpet? No party must join our Kazhagam in an alliance; the opposition vote, which is whirling against the ruling AIADMK faster than a tsunami and multiplying many times over each day, must be scattered; the Kazhagam must be isolated: with this wicked aim, they work with the government's intelligence department, publishing a different imaginary tale every day and continually trying to confuse the public. They themselves add parties to an alliance. They themselves decide how many seats each party will receive. They themselves determine and publish the conditions under which one party will join another. They sow hatred by asking whether such an alliance would not contradict the parties' beliefs and principles. They go out of their way to recruit for the ruling party, while trying to sever and drive away anyone who may join us. In every act, they demonstrate that, for them, blood is thicker than water—“Blood is thicker than Water.” Whatever fraudulent schemes and stratagems they may adopt, the Kazhagam alliance will smash them all, pulverise them and cast them aside, and its victory is certain. Our past history bears witness that we have seized many such plotters, and many challenges that came veiled rather than openly, by the scruff of the neck, shaken them, pushed them aside and continued advancing on our journey towards our ideals. Therefore,
if Udanpirappugal think of magnifying even a small difference between us, we must not forget that the very thought will encourage those whom I described as “a certain few.”

If, even before the election, our own Udanpirappugal begin clashing with one another in a few places, certain people in the media, such as Vaithi and Varadhan, may be driven by their burning resentment to hide from the people's minds the atrocities and grave wrongs of the AIADMK rulers and instead inflate our internal party disputes.

No party must join our Kazhagam in an alliance; the opposition vote, which is whirling against the ruling AIADMK faster than a tsunami and multiplying many times over each day, must be scattered; the Kazhagam must be isolated: with this wicked aim, they work with the government's intelligence department, publishing a different imaginary tale every day and continually trying to confuse the public. They themselves add parties to an alliance. They themselves decide how many seats each party will receive. They themselves determine and publish the conditions under which one party will join another. They sow hatred by asking whether such an alliance would not contradict the parties' beliefs and principles.
Let me remind you of something Anna once said:

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is not merely a political party; it is a movement for social reform. For all those engaged in it, who occupies which place is not the issue; nor is it important who assumes which responsibility at which time. Each person bearing a responsibility is like someone stationed in the battle camp formed by this movement. If enemies attempt to enter it, they will be caught and crushed between the closeness of our ranks.

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is an organisation formed so that each person may work from a particular place. If our opponents imagine that other hierarchies or resentments exist among us, we can only say that they do not know the history of how we grew. The reason we were not all born from one mother's womb is simply that one mother's womb could not have borne so many children.”

Perarignar Anna spoke those moving words at a meeting on Chennai's seafront on 26 September 1960. Our opponents do not know that we have always held them in our memory. The enemies who wait longingly, wondering whether a split will arise among us and whether they can enter between us and drink our blood, will only be disappointed!

Therefore, even if small differences exist, Udanpirappugal of the Kazhagam must forget them and remember the saying, “If one looks only for faults, no kinship will remain.” Keeping Anna's sari analogy in mind, they must act with the generous spirit that says: if this person served as secretary this time, let another assume that responsibility next time. I caution you that this is how the Kazhagam's Udanpirappugal must understand matters and work. If, instead, events unfold in the spirit of “we will clash, whatever happens,” they will harm both those who clash and this movement, which is their own home. I ask you never to let slip from your memory that “if unity departs, decline comes to all.”

Therefore, I lovingly invite the Kazhagam's Udanpirappugal who come for candidate interviews from tomorrow onward to carry these thoughts in their hearts. Our central objective must be that the DMK wins and that the candidate supported by the DMK wins—not that we personally must win. If we do not receive this opportunity now, a greater opportunity may come later. Even if it never comes to us personally, the victory secured by the Kazhagam is the victory secured by every Udanpirappu of the Kazhagam. Come to the interview with that noble purpose and confidence.
With affection,
M. K.
21 February 2016
